topics.title: Job queue, worker loop, and cancellation
reasons:
Page B is meaningfully stronger on every criterion.

**Behavioral depth**: B surfaces several concrete runtime behaviors that A omits or leaves vague: `parallelPageLimit` defaults to 2 (clamped 1–5, sourced from `src/lib/server/config.ts`); the SSE stream only emits an event when `progress` or `message` actually changes, not on every 500 ms tick; CLI processes have a 10-minute timeout; Codex passes the prompt via stdin rather than as a CLI argument to avoid OS arg-length limits; and — critically — killed CLI subprocesses can resurface as retryable provider errors because the abort signal is not threaded into the provider retry loop. That last point is a genuine operational gotcha that A misses entirely.

**Grounding**: B includes the raw SQL schema and the TypeScript `Job` interface, making the data model verifiable from one place. It also cites `src/lib/server/config.ts` for the parallelPageLimit range and names the Pino child loggers with their component strings. A's snippets are real but less complete.

**Connectedness**: B explicitly flags that the resume route skips the `getActiveJobForRepo()` guard that generation and sync both respect, making the deduplication asymmetry visible and actionable. It explains why the SSE endpoint polls the DB instead of using in-memory pub/sub (Vite HMR resilience). It traces how `wiki_id` gets linked to a job at different points per job type, and how the home page vs. wiki viewer each consume job state. A covers these areas more shallowly.

**Clarity**: Both pages are well-structured and readable. B's "Operational Limitations" section gives a developer a crisp mental model of where the system is intentionally simple and what that implies — exactly the kind of load-bearing context a new contributor needs.

A is solid but scoped narrowly to sync/resume/cancellation and misses behavioral specifics that B captures across the full queue lifecycle.

Page A better covers the stated topic — "Job queue, worker loop, and cancellation" — across all four criteria.

**Behavioral depth**: Page A is more complete. It explains the full worker lifecycle including the 1000ms poll interval and `processing` boolean guard, all three job types, startup stale-job handling, the SSE 500ms polling interval, failure semantics at both the job and page level (a key distinction Page B omits), token accounting, and active-job deduplication differences between the three job-creation routes — including the important asymmetry that resume does not deduplicate. Page B goes deeper on sync-handler internals (10MB diff buffer, 30-second git timeout, 20-commit message capture, `noChangesNeeded` model response) and has a useful edge-cases section, but those are narrower details within one handler.

**Grounding**: Both pages anchor claims in real file paths and code. Page A includes the full SQL schema, the `claimNextJob` atomic update, `cancelStaleJobs`, the terminal-state SSE fast path, and the `completeJobWithTokens` vs `completeJob` branching logic — all verifiable specifics. Page B's sync-specific numbers (10MB, 30s, 20 messages) are grounded but limited to one handler.

**Connectedness**: Page A traces the full system path — jobs table → worker → handler dispatch → per-page p-limit concurrency → SSE route → `JobProgress.svelte` → home page → wiki viewer — and explains how each part interacts. Page B is well-connected within the sync/resume paths but doesn't cover how the UI surfaces jobs on the home page or wiki viewer, and omits token/cost accounting end-to-end.

**Operational limitations**: Page A's explicit limitations section (single-process `processing` flag, SQLite polling, startup cancellation semantics, provider-wide process kills, retry-loop interaction with abort signal, page failure vs job failure independence, weaker resume deduplication) is the clearest statement of what a developer must understand to operate and extend this system safely. Page B surfaces some of these inline but never synthesizes them.

Page B's sync-handler depth (rescanChangedFiles internals, `noChangesNeeded` handling, edge-case section) is genuinely useful, but it covers a sub-path rather than the system as a whole. For the topic as scoped, Page A is the stronger reference.

topics.title: Wiki generation pipeline (outline and pages)
reasons: Page B wins on every weighted criterion. On behavioral depth: B covers the complete pipeline including markdown cleanup (four distinct post-processing steps: title heading removal, leaky heading sanitization, diagram enforcement, link rewriting), page context retrieval with the 100,000-character fallback truncation, structured output schema differences between Claude CLI and Codex CLI, and explicit failure/resume behavior — all absent or truncated in A. On grounding: B adds concrete, verifiable thresholds that A omits (outline scaling by file count ≤5/≤20/≤50/>50, overviewMinNonDocFiles=2, overviewMaxDocRatio=0.5, lowComplexityFileThreshold=2, pricing per-million-to-per-thousand conversion logic, the 100k char truncation, test file references). Page A's mermaid diagram is also visibly cut off mid-node, a notable defect. On connectedness: B closes with an explicit "How This Connects to the Rest of the System" section and names the precise module boundaries (git.ts, scanner.ts, worker.ts, SvelteKit routes). On clarity: both are well-written, but B's complete flow diagram and accurate claims about the absence of an agentic per-page mode (correcting a potential misconception) add meaningful signal. Page A is a solid overview but stops well short of what B delivers.

Page A is the stronger page on all four criteria. On behavioral depth, it covers critical details Page B either omits or cuts off: the file-count scaling thresholds for outline size (<=5 / <=20 / <=50 / >50), what validateOutline explicitly does NOT check (zero sections, zero pages per section, missing descriptions), the note that only a root-level "readme.md" or "readme" path is selected (not nested READMEs), the full Markdown cleanup pipeline (title heading stripping, leaky-heading sanitization, diagram deduplication, link rewriting with GitHub vs local behavior), page-level failure tolerance (wiki still marked completed even with failed pages), and the resume workflow's behavior of using current model rather than the original. Page B is cut off mid-Mermaid diagram, losing whatever followed the concurrency section. Both pages are well-grounded with real file paths and plausible code snippets, but Page A's explicit constants (maxFilePathsPerPage=25, parallelPageLimit default 2 clamped 1–5, 100_000-char context truncation, 10-minute outline timeout) and its explanation of what the normalizer's thresholds mean in practice give it a clear edge. Page A's "How This Connects to the Rest of the System" section is also more explicit about the pipeline boundary than anything Page B has. Page B covers embedding refresh in slightly more detail, but that does not offset the gaps introduced by truncation and the missing post-processing coverage.

topics.title: Embeddings and retrieval modes
reasons: Page A directly covers the retrieval mode decision logic (constrained vs hybrid_auto, weakness detection thresholds, global fallback, merge/dedupe), the complete runtime data flow from query assembly through prompt injection, and every failure/fallback branch with actual code. Page B is equally well-grounded but documents the indexing pipeline (chunking, batch writes, ANN manifest), which is complementary but off-topic for "retrieval modes."

Both pages are genuinely strong — real file paths, verifiable code, concrete values, and honest failure-handling coverage throughout. The margin is narrow but real.

Page B wins on the criteria that matter most here:

**Behavioral depth on the titled topic.** The page title is "Embeddings and retrieval modes." Page B owns the retrieval-modes half: it fully explains constrained vs hybrid_auto, the weakness-detection algorithm with all four thresholds (minChunks=3, minContextChars=4000, minTopScore=0.3, minScoreGap=0.05), why global fallback fires, how merge/dedupe works, and the full-file path with its 100K cap. Page A is an excellent indexing reference but doesn't touch retrieval modes at all — it explicitly defers that topic to "the Context Retrieval page."

**Connectedness.** Page B's "How Components Interact" section traces the full chain from generator query assembly through retrieval config resolution, constrained/global scoring, merge, formatting, and back into the generation prompt. It also precisely marks the ANN boundary (searchAnn exists but current page generation doesn't call it — exact cosine scan instead). Page A connects well within its scope but stops at "the embedding index is built; see the other page for retrieval."

**Query-assembly behavior.** Page B surfaces something neither page's title hints at: how the retrieval query is constructed (sectionTitle + pageTitle + description + filePaths for new pages; title + changeTitle + changeDescription + diff[:2000] + filePaths for updates). That's directly runtime-observable behavior that a developer debugging retrieval quality needs.

Page A is the stronger reference for the indexing pipeline itself — its chunking strategy, refresh-selection logic, atomic replacement transaction, and ANN manifest details are more thorough than anything Page B says about those topics. If the comparison were "Embeddings Indexing" vs "Context Retrieval," this would be a tie by scope. Judged against the shared topic "Embeddings and retrieval modes," Page B covers the behavioral core of that topic more completely.

topics.title: Repository scanning and filtering
reasons:
Page B wins on behavioral depth and connectedness, the two most important criteria.

Behavioral depth: Page B covers two significant runtime behaviors that Page A omits entirely. First, the `skipDataPrep` commit-SHA comparison in `handleFullGeneration()` — the code path where scanning still runs but document writes are skipped when the HEAD SHA matches the stored one — is a non-obvious optimization central to understanding when documents are actually updated. Page A describes the upsert but never explains the condition that gates it. Second, Page B covers the sync flow in detail: the `last_commit_sha` requirement (local repos without it must be regenerated), the early-exit when SHA is unchanged, `getDiffSinceCommit()` and its 10 MB maxBuffer cap, and the delete-then-reinsert pattern for changed paths. Page A mentions sync only to disclaim responsibility for it.

Page B also surfaces a genuine implementation subtlety: the `.min.js`/`.min.css` entries in `SKIP_EXTENSIONS` are ineffective because `extname()` returns `.js`/`.css` for those filenames — those files fall through to the average-line-length heuristic instead. That kind of grounded behavioral observation is exactly what helps a developer understand how the system actually works.

Connectedness: Page B explicitly names the three artifacts the layer hands off (updated `repos` row, `documents` rows, in-memory `ScannedFile[]`) and traces each forward to outline generation, page creation, embedding refresh, and sync. It also identifies the local-repo sync limitation — that `handleSync()` calls `cloneRepo()` rather than `prepareLocalRepo()`, so local-directory sync is not a real implementation. Page A's "Boundaries and Responsibilities" section lists responsibilities but does not show how the outputs are consumed.

Both pages are well-grounded with real file paths, concrete thresholds (1 MiB, 10 000 files, 8 KB binary sample, 500-char minification threshold), and verifiable code snippets. Both are clear and readable. The gap is on depth and connectedness.

Page A wins on behavioral depth and connectedness. Its decisive advantages: (1) it explains the `skipDataPrep` logic — that scanning still runs when the commit SHA matches but documents are not rewritten — which is a critical runtime behavior Page B omits; (2) it covers the sync flow in detail: `getDiffSinceCommit`, the 40-char hex SHA validation regex, the 10 MB `maxBuffer` on diffs, the 20-commit log cap, and `rescanChangedFiles` including the key invariant that full generation does not delete disappeared files while sync does; (3) it flags the `extname()` edge case where `.min.js` entries in `SKIP_EXTENSIONS` are ineffective because `extname("bundle.min.js")` returns `.js` — a specific, verifiable subtlety; (4) it identifies the local-repository sync limitation (sync calls `cloneRepo()` rather than `prepareLocalRepo()`, so local-directory wikis cannot actually sync). Page B self-limits to "stops at the boundary where DeepWiki has produced ScannedFile objects," which is a legitimate scope choice but leaves out exactly the behavioral depth and cross-system connections that matter most to a developer working in this area. Where both pages cover the same ground (input parsing, filtering rules, thresholds, authentication sanitization), they are equally well-grounded. The difference is not prose style; Page A simply explains more of how the system actually behaves.

topics.title: AI provider invocation (Claude/Codex CLIs)
reasons: Page B wins on behavioral depth and connectedness, the two highest-weight criteria. It traces the full stack — routes → queue handlers → generator → provider → CLIs → DB — while Page A stays mostly within the adapter layer. Page B's additional coverage is substantive, not padding: it shows the three distinct generation paths (outline uses text JSON parsing, pages use PAGE_SCHEMA structured output, updates use UPDATE_SCHEMA with nullable content), explains the parallelPageLimit clamping (settings accept up to 10, effective config clamps to 5), reveals how cost accounting flows from raw token counts through calculateCost() into per-page wiki_pages columns and per-job totals, explains the full cancellation mechanism (AbortController prevents new work while kill functions stop running CLIs), names the exact DB columns that store usage metadata, and explains the architectural decision to not supply Codex a cwd or tool allowlist — context is injected by DeepWiki, not retrieved by the model. Page B also calls out a non-obvious failure-handling nuance: individual pages can fail while the job still completes. Both pages are equally well-grounded with real file paths and plausible code snippets; Page A's summary table is useful but not sufficient to overcome Page B's deeper system-level coverage.

Page A wins on behavioral depth and connectedness, which matter most here. Its "Structured Output in Generator Code" section explains how the provider layer is *actually used* — three distinct calling patterns with different schemas (outline uses text + extractJson, page uses a content schema with structured-output fallback, update uses a nullable no-change schema) — and why they differ. Page B inventories the same files but omits this caller-side behavior entirely. Page A also traces the full pipeline: routes → queue handlers → generator → provider → DB columns, including the accumulateUsage/completeJobWithTokens logic and which DB columns receive per-page vs. job-level token data. Page A explains the architectural decision behind context injection (Codex gets no cwd or tool permissions, so DeepWiki controls what the model sees). Page B's advantages — the full normalizeSchema() and extractPotentialJson() implementations, the summary comparison table, test code excerpts, and logging details — are real but secondary: they describe implementation mechanics rather than runtime behavior and data flow. Both pages are well-grounded with real file paths and plausible values; neither invents anything suspicious. The summary table in Page B is genuinely useful, but it doesn't compensate for missing the generator-level calling conventions, which is the most important behavioral story in this area of the codebase.

topics.title: Sync, resume, and incremental updates
reasons: Page B is superior on all three top criteria. On behavioral depth, it provides concrete operational thresholds Page A omits or undersells: the diff is truncated to 50,000 characters inside `buildUpdatePrompt()`; full-file context is truncated at 100,000 characters; `parallelPageLimit` defaults to 2, clamped 1–5; the scanner caps at 10,000 files and 1,048,576 bytes per file; the embedding indexer skips files whose content hash is already indexed. It also surfaces the important asymmetry that the resume route does not call `getActiveJobForRepo()`, so multiple resume jobs can pile up, whereas Page A only notes sync deduplicates. On grounding, Page B backs up more claims with specific numbers pulled from real config constants. On connectedness, Page B adds an entire section on how both workflows feed into `retrieveContextForPrompt()`—covering both the embedding path and the full-file fallback—and explicitly names the schema cascade rules (`ON DELETE CASCADE`, `ON DELETE SET NULL`) that govern data cleanup during rescan. It also flags the local-wiki sync limitation (sync calls `cloneRepo(owner, repoName)` instead of `prepareLocalRepo()`) as a failure case, which is directly useful. Page A's Mermaid diagram is a readability plus, but it does not compensate for the missing thresholds and retrieval-integration coverage.

Page A wins on behavioral depth and grounding. It consistently supplies concrete, verifiable thresholds that Page B omits entirely: `maxFileSize` (1 MB), `maxFilesPerRepo` (10,000), `parallelPageLimit` clamped to 1–5 with a default of 2, diff truncated to 50,000 characters inside `buildUpdatePrompt()`, full-file context truncated at 100,000 characters, and embedding hybrid_auto defaults of top-K 10 and max context 16,000 chars. These numbers let a developer predict and reason about system behavior in ways Page B cannot.

Page A also covers two areas Page B skips: (1) the full context-retrieval path—how `retrieveContextForPrompt()` is called by both `generatePageUpdate()` and `generatePage()`, what happens when embeddings fail (fallback to direct file retrieval), and how deleted files disappear from context after `rescanChangedFiles()`; (2) the local-wiki limitation, where `cloneRepo(owner, repoName)` is called unconditionally, so a wiki generated from a local path cannot be synced.

Page B is stronger on cancellation—it shows the full DELETE route, the `throwIfCancelled` implementation, the `cancelJob` SQL predicate, and the client-side SSE handler including the cancel button—and its sequence diagram aids orientation. But its embedding-failure claim ("do not appear to abort sync here unless…") is a hedged, uncertain statement, which is a grounding penalty. The cancellation advantage does not outweigh Page A's superior threshold coverage, retrieval-chain explanation, and documented local-wiki edge case.

topics.title: Database schema and persistence
reasons: Page B is a clear winner on all four criteria. On behavioral depth: Page A is truncated mid-sentence in the wikis section and omits wiki_pages, jobs, route/worker interactions, delete/cascade behavior, and testing coverage entirely. Page B covers all of those, and adds critical runtime details absent from A — the `last_commit_sha` sync failure condition with the exact error message from handlers.ts, `cancelStaleJobs()` startup behavior, COALESCE semantics in `createWikiPage()` for preserving existing content during upsert, `parallelPageLimit` being accepted 1–10 by the API but clamped to 1–5 by `getEffectiveConfig()`, and version assignment inside a SQLite transaction to prevent races. On grounding: both use real file paths and code, but B adds the TypeScript status unions (the exact string literals for `Wiki.status`, `Job.type`, etc.), the full settings key inventory (including `retrievalModeGeneration`, `weakness*` keys, `generationModel`, `showRepoOwner` absent from A), and the 500 ms SSE polling interval. On connectedness: B has an explicit "Route and Worker Interactions" section showing the 10-step generation write order and explaining exactly how routes create jobs while the worker mutates wikis/pages/embeddings — A never reaches this. On clarity: both are readable, but A's abrupt truncation makes it incomplete for any practical use.

Page A wins on every weighted criterion. On behavioral depth, it covers details Page B lacks or truncates: the 1-second worker polling loop with an in-memory `processing` guard, `cancelStaleJobs()` running at startup from `src/hooks.server.ts`, the exact failure message when `last_commit_sha` is missing, the 10-step full-generation write order, the `ON DELETE SET NULL` exception for `jobs.wiki_id` and why it matters for job history, the `parallelPageLimit` being clamped to 1–5 in `getEffectiveConfig()`, `listWikis()` joining only the latest completed job to prevent duplicate rows, and cross-repo version leak protection in `getWikiByOwnerRepoVersion()`. On connectedness, Page A traces data flow from routes through the worker to each table, including how sync differs from full generation and how resume skips document scanning entirely. Page B has one advantage (noting the bun:sqlite vs better-sqlite3 split between tests and production), but it is cut off mid-sentence in the wikis section—the wikis table description ends with "source_type distinguishes `"—making it structurally incomplete. Page A's grounding is equally strong with the same real file paths and plausible values throughout.

topics.title: Wiki viewer and UI
reasons: Page A focuses entirely on the wiki viewer and UI, delivering substantially more behavioral depth on every dimension that matters for this topic. It explains runtime mechanics that Page B only names: how SSE polling opens, updates, and closes on terminal states; how the iOS scroll-lock works (body position fixed at scroll offset, not just overflow:hidden); how the home-page dedup key is constructed (`id:{repo_id}` vs `nm:{source_type}:{owner}/{repo}`); how the IntersectionObserver rootMargin (`-80px 0px -80% 0px`) determines the active heading; how duplicate heading IDs get numeric suffixes; how absolute filesystem paths in generated links are suffix-matched against known repo files and rewritten; and how Mermaid sanitization handles overly-long arrows and bracket-label punctuation. Page A also documents failure handling explicitly — DOMPurify config, Mermaid render fallback, SSE parse-error behavior, non-fatal API failures — and names concrete storage keys (`deepwiki:home-view`). Page B covers the same components but at a high-level inventory pass, spending most of its length on server-side concerns (embeddings, scanner, git pipeline, database schema) that are off-topic for "Wiki viewer and UI." Both pages are well-grounded with real file paths and verifiable code, but Page A's snippets are more complete and directly illustrate the behavior being described. For a developer trying to understand how the viewer and rendering pipeline actually work, Page A is unambiguously more useful.

Page B is clearly better for the "Wiki viewer and UI" topic on every weighted criterion.

Behavioral depth: Page B explains actual runtime behavior at every layer — the `lastSelectionRouteKey` deduplication guard on page selection, the iOS PWA scroll-lock workaround (fixing body position to current scroll offset), IntersectionObserver rootMargin of "-80px 0px -80% 0px", the duplicate-heading numeric-suffix algorithm, DOMPurify configuration (ADD_TAGS/ADD_ATTR), the image-to-code fallback for GitHub blob URLs that don't serve raw images, SSE parse-error handling, and the UTC-normalization bug in browser date parsing for timezone-naive SQLite timestamps. Page A inventories the same components but describes almost none of this behavior.

Grounding: Both pages cite real file paths and plausible code. Page B goes further — it includes actual localStorage key names (`deepwiki:home-view`), specific Mermaid sanitization regex patterns, DOMPurify option objects, and the three-choice duplicate-wiki prompt flow — all of which are concretely verifiable.

Connectedness: Page B traces the full interaction path for each major UI scenario (start generation → SSE → job card removal → wiki reload; viewer load → slug resolution → WikiTree → WikiPage → TOC headings; sidebar actions → job overlay → route reload). Page A has one combined "how the pieces fit" section that covers the whole repo and gives the UI only a final sentence.

Page A is a competent broad repository map, but for this specific topic it reads as an inventory. Page B reads as documentation of how the UI actually works.
